reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Clear, opinionated 'When to use / When NOT to use' distinction with concrete, non-circular criteria — the CDN comparison is exactly the decision support beginners need.
Multi-region patterns (active-active, failover, region-per-audience) are developed to equal depth with tradeoffs, not just enumerated.
Cross-region data strategy table (DynamoDB global tables / Aurora Global Database / S3 CRR / application-level sync) with consistency and use-case columns is highly scannable and citable.

FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (same stage name in multiple regions?), AWS service use-case (how to keep databases in sync?), and comparison (multi-region vs CDN?). Ten entries, no filler.
Shell commands are split correctly — one command per fenced bash block for deploy, delete, and debug:logs. No copy-button trap.
Guardrails example is well-grounded in the deploy source (validateGuardrails is called early before any CloudFormation work) and shows a realistic allowedRegions list.
The 'most teams don't need this' framing is appropriately opinionated and calibrated to beginners who don't yet know if they need it.
problems: The TypeScript config example uses `params.region` (in `defineConfig(({ params }) => { const isPrimary = params.region === 'eu-west-1'; ... })`), but the provided source (`src/commands/deploy/index.ts`) stores region in `globalStateManager.args` — not in user-defined deploy-time params. If `params` in defineConfig is restricted to user-declared parameters (not built-in system values), the example is silently broken. A developer who copy-pastes it and runs it may get `undefined` at runtime with no obvious error.

The claim 'Stacktape internally hashes region + stack name + AWS account ID to produce a globally unique identifier that prevents resource-naming collisions across regions' is not grounded in the provided source file. The source shows stack naming via `globalStateManager.targetStack.stackName` but no hashing logic is visible. Stating an internal implementation detail that cannot be verified builds false confidence.

The statement 'This validation runs at the start of every deploy, preview, and dev command' is only confirmed for the deploy path in the provided source. Preview-changes and dev-mode source files are not in the inline source set — the claim for those commands is unverified.

The `<FlowDiagram>` MDX component is used in the GitOps section but is not listed in any MDX component guidance or example page. If it is not part of the Stacktape docs component palette, the section renders as raw JSX or breaks the page entirely.

The '21 AWS regions' count in prose and the region table are not grounded in the provided source. The table itself counts to 21 (6 Americas + 6 Europe + 7 Asia Pacific + 2 MEA), but whether this list is current or accurate is not verifiable from deploy/index.ts alone.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the `params.region` usage in the TypeScript defineConfig example. Verify whether the Stacktape defineConfig callback exposes a built-in `params.region` field. If it does not, replace with the correct mechanism — for example, an explicit deploy-time parameter declared in the config that the user passes via `--param region=eu-west-1`, or a condition on `process.env` if that is the supported pattern. A non-working primary code example is a critical practical failure for a beginner audience.

Remove or reframe the 'Stacktape internally hashes region + stack name + AWS account ID' sentence — it is not grounded in the provided source. Replace with a source-verifiable statement: the stacks share the same logical CloudFormation stack name pattern but reside in separate regions and cannot interact directly. Do not assert internal implementation details that are not in the source.

Verify `<FlowDiagram>` is a valid Stacktape MDX component. If it is not, replace the GitOps section visual with prose paragraphs or a numbered list. An unrecognised component silently breaks the page for readers.
optionalImprovements: Soften the 21-region count claim or add a note pointing to the Stacktape docs or console for the current supported-region list. Region support can change and a hardcoded count will go stale.

The 'Supported regions' table is useful, but for a deployment concept page aimed at beginners, it sits between 'How it works' and 'Automate with GitOps' and interrupts the flow. Consider moving it to the bottom as a reference section, or collapsing it into the FAQ ('Which AWS regions does Stacktape support?').

The guardrail `type: 'region-restriction'` code example is solid, but the surrounding prose says the validation runs 'at the start of every deploy, preview, and dev command' — narrow this to 'every deploy command' unless the other command sources confirm it.

The 'Sharing configuration across regions' section introduces `params.region` in code but the surrounding prose doesn't explain the deploy-time parameters system or link to /deployment-and-lifecycle/deploy-time-parameters before using it. Add one sentence: 'Pass the region as a deploy-time parameter via `--param region=eu-west-1` and declare it in your config — see [Deploy-time parameters](/deployment-and-lifecycle/deploy-time-parameters).'

The 'Automate with GitOps' section mentions 'Promise.all' in the FlowDiagram description — this is an internal implementation detail that is unlikely to be meaningful or reassuring to a beginner and could be removed regardless of whether the component renders.

The monitoring section transitions directly from 'Use `stacktape debug:logs`...' into two back-to-back command blocks with no prose between them distinguishing which block is which — the reader has to infer context from the flag values alone.
mandatoryFixes: Verify that `params.region` is a valid accessor in Stacktape's `defineConfig` callback before publishing. If it is not, replace the example with the documented alternative — for example, using `process.env.STP_STAGE` or a deploy-time parameter ref — and update surrounding prose to match. A non-functional central code example on a multi-region deployment page is a high-severity practical failure.
optionalImprovements: Replace `<FlowDiagram>` with standard MDX — a short bulleted list or numbered steps — unless the component is confirmed as part of the docs component library. This removes the risk of a broken render with no fallback.

Qualify or remove the 'Stacktape internally generates a globally unique hash from region + stack name + AWS account ID' sentence. Either cite the source that confirms it or replace it with 'each region gets its own CloudFormation stack; resource names are scoped to the region and account by Stacktape's naming conventions' which is defensible without the specific hash claim.

Scope the 'Supported regions' table caveat — note that the region list reflects what Stacktape validates at deploy time rather than stating a permanent 21-region count, since AWS adds regions periodically.

Add one sentence of prose between the two monitoring command blocks to distinguish them — e.g. 'For the EU region:' before the first block and 'For the US region:' before the second. Same pattern for the deletion section.

Consider shortening 'Sharing configuration across regions' → 'Region-specific config', 'Monitoring multi-region stacks' → 'Monitoring', and 'Deleting multi-region stacks' → 'Deletion' to better fit the 1–5 word noun-form heading guideline.

The active-passive failover section notes '30–90 seconds' for DNS TTL plus health-check detection time — add a caveat that this depends on Route 53 health-check interval settings (which are AWS-configurable), so readers don't treat the range as fixed.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page is easy to scan, with clear H2s, separate “When to use” and “When NOT to use” sections, tables for region/data comparisons, and a broad FAQ.
The Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense> with TypeScript tabs and class-based resources, avoiding the forbidden YAML and StacktapeConfig patterns.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and link the first `stacktape debug:logs` mention correctly.
problems: The draft is far beyond the provided source. The page blurs CLI and Console behavior. Console-specific claims about GitOps configurations, stage entries, alert channels, and adding/removing regional configs are not grounded because no `console-app` source is provided.

The human-maintained instruction asks for deploy-time state details where state matters, but the page mostly discusses failures generically and does not ground CloudFormation states from the provided deploy source. The only visible state-specific detail in the source is handling around `UPDATE_FAILED`.
The `How it works` section contains useful structure, but several details are unsupported by the provided deploy source, so an AI answer engine could extract inaccurate or unverifiable snippets.

mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground every Stacktape-specific claim not supported by `src/commands/deploy/index.ts`. In this source set, keep claims narrow: deploy initializes stack services, validates guardrails, packages artifacts, uploads artifacts, performs full CloudFormation deploy or hotswap, refreshes stack info after full deploy, invalidates CDN caches when configured, and reports success.
Delete or heavily qualify the “Supported regions” table unless a source file defining valid regions is provided. Do not claim 21 supported regions from the current source.

Remove the GitOps Console flow and Console-specific monitoring claims unless matching `console-app` or GitOps command/source files are provided. Distinguish CLI behavior from Console behavior explicitly.

Remove unsupported claims about AWS_DEFAULT_REGION, `stacktape defaults:configure`, stack-name hash formulas, Route 53 routing policies created or not created by Stacktape, and custom-domain `disableDnsRecordCreation` unless the relevant source files are provided.

Revise the guardrail section to avoid claiming the `region-restriction` schema and preview/dev validation behavior from this source. The deploy source only shows `validateGuardrails({ guardrails: configManager.guardrails, hasConfig: true })` during deploy.

verifierResults.summary: The draft makes many Stacktape-specific claims that are not grounded in the only provided source file. The strongest issues are unsupported CLI region behavior, invented multi-region internals, unsupported GitOps/Console flows, unsupported region and guardrail details, and a few claims that overstate behavior beyond `deploy` source evidence.

issues.statement: “The `--region` flag controls which AWS region the CloudFormation stack is created in. To deploy the same application to two regions, run two deploy commands targeting different regions.”
issues.reason: The provided `deploy` command source does not show a `--region` flag, region argument handling, or any logic proving that the same stage can be deployed to multiple regions as independent stacks. This is central CLI behavior and needs grounding in CLI command/config source, not inference.
issues.suggestedFix: Either cite/source the CLI argument definition and stack identity logic, or narrow the page to source-grounded wording such as: deployments operate on `globalStateManager.targetStack.stackName` and the deploy command runs a CloudFormation deploy for the selected target stack. Do not document `--region` behavior unless the relevant CLI/global-state source is provided.

issues.statement: “Internally, Stacktape hashes region + stack name + AWS account ID to produce a globally unique identifier that prevents resource-naming collisions across regions.”
issues.reason: The provided deploy source does not contain the claimed hash formula or cross-region collision-prevention behavior. The only naming-related behavior visible here is a warning when project+stage stack name is too long and some resource names are obfuscated.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the hash formula unless the naming/global-state source is provided. A cautious replacement could mention only the sourced behavior: Stacktape warns when the project+stage stack name is too long and resource names may be obfuscated.

issues.statement: “Stacktape supports 21 AWS regions,” followed by a complete supported-region table.
issues.reason: The provided source file contains no supported-region list, region enum, or validation source. A hard-coded count and table would directly affect user deployment choices and must be grounded in CLI/config validation source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the supported-region table or replace it with a cautious link/statement only after adding the authoritative region source. If no region source is available, say the supported region set is not documented in the provided source for this page.

issues.statement: “Create two GitOps configurations watching the same branch but targeting different regions — both deployments start in parallel when a push arrives,” and the FlowDiagram claim that “Both deployments start simultaneously via Promise.all.”
issues.reason: The provided source is the CLI deploy command. It does not include GitOps configuration behavior, GitHub App webhook matching, or any multi-region parallel deployment orchestration. The visible `Promise.all` calls are unrelated to GitOps fan-out.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the GitOps implementation details unless console/GitOps server source is provided. Keep only a cautious CI pattern such as running separate deploy jobs per target region if the CLI region behavior is separately sourced.

issues.statement: The `region-restriction` guardrail example and the claim: “This validation runs at the start of every deploy, preview, and dev command.”
issues.reason: The deploy source only proves that `validateGuardrails` is called during `deploy`. It does not prove the existence or shape of a `region-restriction` guardrail, the `allowedRegions` property, or validation in preview/dev commands.
issues.suggestedFix: Limit the behavior claim to deploy only, or add authoritative guardrail type and preview/dev command sources. Do not show the `region-restriction` config example unless the guardrail schema/type source is provided.

issues.statement: “In the Stacktape Console, each region appears as a separate stage entry. You can configure alert channels per region so the right team gets notified about region-specific incidents.”
issues.reason: No Console source is provided. The deploy source only constructs a Console URL using project and stage; it does not establish region display behavior, stage entries per region, or per-region alert-channel configuration.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove Console UI behavior claims, or replace them with a generic link to relevant Console/observability pages without describing screen organization or per-region configuration.

issues.statement: “A failure in one region does not affect others — the failed region rolls back via CloudFormation's automatic rollback (if enabled), while successful regions continue serving traffic.”
issues.reason: The deploy source shows rollback-related artifact cleanup when auto rollback is enabled, but it does not establish multi-region independence or that other regions continue serving traffic. It also does not describe CloudFormation automatic rollback behavior in the user-facing way stated.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow this to the sourced deploy behavior: when a deploy error occurs and auto rollback is enabled, Stacktape deletes artifacts from the rolled-back deploy except for stack monitoring errors. Avoid claiming impact on other regions unless multi-region orchestration source is provided.

issues.statement: “Stacktape creates simple Route 53 alias records per region via custom domains,” and “set `disableDnsRecordCreation: true` on your custom domain config.”
issues.reason: The provided deploy source does not include custom-domain resource types, Route 53 record behavior, or the `disableDnsRecordCreation` property. These configuration details may be true elsewhere, but they are not grounded in the supplied source for this verification task.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove these custom-domain implementation details unless custom-domain type/source files are provided. A safer version is to say multi-region DNS routing must be designed separately and link to the custom domains page without naming unsupported properties.

positiveFindings: The draft uses separate fenced bash blocks for separate CLI commands, avoiding the copy-button issue with alternative commands.
The first mention of `stacktape debug:logs` links to `/cli/debug-logs`, matching the documented route in the navigation index.

Internally, Stacktape hashes region + stack name + AWS account ID to produce a globally unique identifier that prevents resource-naming collisions across regions.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong answer-first opening paragraph that clearly explains Stacktape multi-region deployments and why they matter.

The page gives practical operational framing around latency, compliance, DNS routing, data replication, monitoring, and deletion order.

Unsupported claims include: Stacktape supports exactly 20 AWS regions, the CLI region resolution priority, region-derived unique hashes, defineConfig params shape, multi-region DNS behavior, custom-domain behavior, GitOps multi-region behavior, alert-channel scoping, and region-restriction error text.

The active-active, active-passive, and region-per-audience sections are useful but mostly unsupported by the single source file, so they read more like inferred architecture advice than source-grounded Stacktape documentation.

The draft does not distinguish source-grounded CLI behavior from Console behavior carefully enough. The GitOps with Console section describes multi-region Console configuration without console-app source support.

Some concrete numbers are unsupported by the provided source, including under ~200 ms, 30-90 seconds, under 1 second Aurora lag, 400+ edge locations, 2-3 regions, and exactly 20 supported AWS regions.

The FAQ is broad and useful for search, but several answers repeat unsupported claims from the body, especially custom domains, defineConfig params, region count, rollback behavior, and CloudFront edge count.
